text.title: Go Project Structure Failure Modes
text.summary: Antipatterns observed in real go-project-structure deployments. Each pairs symptom + root-cause + fix.

antipattern.symptom: Interface UserRepository defined inside internal/repository; service must import repository just to depend on it.
antipattern.root-cause: Author inverted Go idiom — interface lives at producer, not consumer.
antipattern.fix: Move the interface to the service file. Repository package only provides concrete impl.

antipattern.symptom: var DB *sql.DB at package level; handlers reach for the global directly; tests cannot mock.
antipattern.root-cause: Author copy-pasted tutorial code that uses globals for brevity.
antipattern.fix: Constructor injection. Pass *sql.DB into handlers via struct field; parallel tests get isolated instances.

antipattern.symptom: pkg/ directory holds private helpers and business logic that no external module imports.
antipattern.root-cause: Author treats pkg/ as 'public-by-convention'; once code is there, moving it to internal/ is a breaking change.
antipattern.fix: Reject pkg/ unless an external module is named that imports from it. Default to internal/.

antipattern.symptom: main.go calls srv.Shutdown(ctx) but leaks worker goroutines and the DB pool stays open.
antipattern.root-cause: Author treated srv.Shutdown as a complete shutdown signal.
antipattern.fix: Drain background goroutines via ctx.Done(); db.Close() after; signal each consumer to quit explicitly.

antipattern.symptom: Dockerfile lacks USER directive; binary runs as root in the runtime stage.
antipattern.root-cause: Generated Dockerfile copied without prod hardening.
antipattern.fix: Add USER nobody:nobody; switch runtime stage to distroless or scratch.
